source_texts: The hours passed very slowly. It was about half-past eleven when he distinctly heard light, furtive footsteps in the room next to his. Christine was not in her bed, then! Without thinking, Raoul dressed, taking care not to make a sound. And then he waited expectantly. What did he expect? He had no idea. His heart leaped when he heard Christine’s door slowly turn on its hinges. Where could she be going at this hour, when the whole of Perros was asleep? He quietly opened his door and glimpsed her white figure cautiously slipping down the corridor in the moonlight. She reached the staircase and, as she descended, Raoul leaned over the banister. He heard two hurried voices. He only caught one sentence: ‘Don’t lose the key.’ It was the landlady’s voice. Someone opened the door that gave on to the waterfront and locked it again. All went quiet. Raoul rushed back to his room and quickly opened the window. The white figure of Christine stood on the deserted quayside.

The truth is that no one has ever really explained how Joseph Buquet met his death. The verdict at the inquest was ‘suicide under natural circumstances’. In his Memoirs of a Theatre Director, Armand Moncharmin, one of the co-directors who succeeded Messrs Debienne and Poligny, relates the incident as follows: A most unfortunate accident ruined the little party that Messrs Debienne and Poligny were giving to mark their departure.

We were soon enclosed in rubber garments up to our necks, with air equipment fastened on with shoulder straps. There was no sign of the Ruhmkorff lighting devices. Before placing my head in the copper helmet I mentioned this to the captain.
"Then let me go and meet her," exclaimed Arkady with another access of "magnanimity" as he sprang from his chair. " Yes, let me go and explain to her why she need not shun me."
“She’s not here,” Ivan said irritatedly. “You’re really a crazy old man! Hey, he’s fainted again . . . Smerdyakov, get me some water and a towel. Move!” Smerdyakov hurried out to get some water.

As for the man, he truly was so tired that he did not even make the most of those clean white sheets. He snuffed out his candle with his nostril, the way convicts do, and dropping on to the bed fully dressed, immediately fell into a deep sleep.
The ‘principal tenant’ of Jean Valjean’s day had died and had been replaced by another exactly like her. I am not sure which philosopher said, ‘There is never any shortage of old women.’

K. had just reached the door again when there was a knock. It was the maid, who reported that Fräulein Montag would like a few words with Herr K. and requested that he join her for that purpose in the dining room, where she was awaiting him. K. listened pensively to the maid, then turned with an almost scornful look to a startled Frau Grubach. His look seemed to say he’d long since expected Fräulein Montag’s invitation and that it fit in quite well with the general annoyance he was being forced to suffer at the hands of Frau Grubach’s boarders this Sunday morning. He sent the maid back to say that he would come at once, then went to his wardrobe to change his jacket, responding to Frau Grubach, who was complaining under her breath about the irksome young woman, merely by asking her to please clear away the breakfast dishes. “But you’ve hardly touched anything,” said Frau Grubach. “Oh, just take it away,” cried K; it seemed to him as if Fräulein Montag were somehow mixed up with it all, making it disgusting.

For his part, Conseil thinks he saw a six-foot-wide turtle adorned with three lengthwise ridges. I regret not seeing this reptile, for I thought I recognized the leatherback from the description Conseil gave me — quite a rare species. For my part, I only noticed a few loggerhead turtles with their long carapaces.

That was a new call, and Heidi obeyed quickly. When the child came downstairs in her smart little frock, she opened her eyes wide. " Oh, grandfather!" she exclaimed, "I have never seen you in your Sunday coat with the silver buttons. Oh, how fine you look!"
rationales: Outputs 3 and 4 share distinctive vocabulary choices ('waterfront' and 'dock' instead of 'harbour/quayside'), both expand the text with added interpretive phrases, both use a modernizing style that adds explanations not in the original, and both restructure sentences similarly. The unique word substitution 'dock' for 'jetty/quayside' and 'waterfront' for 'harbour' appear in both, strongly suggesting the same paraphraser.

Outputs 1 and 2 share multiple distinctive linguistic fingerprints: both use the full name 'Armand Moncharmin' (rather than 'M. Moncharmin'), both describe the verdict using the phrasing 'under natural circumstances' combined with self-inflicted death, both refer to him as 'Theatre Director' (vs. 'Theatre Manager'), and both describe the party incident as an 'accident/mishap' that 'disrupted/occurred at' the farewell gathering. These parallel choices in terminology, structure, and phrasing strongly suggest they came from the same paraphraser.

Outputs 0 and 2 both begin with the identical phrase 'Before placing my head in the copper helmet, I mentioned this to the captain.' and follow the same sentence order (helmet → rubber clothing → Ruhmkorff absence). They also share very similar word choices ('air apparatus', 'rubber', reference to neck/necks) and parallel sentence structures, indicating they were produced by the same paraphrasing system.

Outputs 0 and 3 share the same stylistic approach: both render 'magnanimity' as a plain English phrase ('pretended generosity' / 'renewed sense of generosity') rather than keeping the word, both use 'leapt/leaped from his seat/feet', and both end with 'no reason to [reject/avoid] me' — a parallel phrasing distinct from the other outputs. This consistent modernization and paraphrasing pattern points to the same system.

Outputs 0 and 3 share nearly identical structure and phrasing: both begin with '"She's gone," Ivan snapped.', both use 'Look, he's passed out again...', both list 'Smerdyakov, water and a towel,' followed by a single word of urgency, and both end with 'Smerdyakov rushed off to fetch the items.' The only differences are minor word swaps ('mad old fool' vs 'madman', 'now' vs 'quickly', 'really a' vs 'truly a'), which is characteristic of the same paraphraser producing slightly varied outputs.

Outputs 1 and 2 share very similar stylistic patterns: both begin with 'The man was so/extremely exhausted,' both use 'collapsed onto the bed' with 'fully clothed,' both use 'instantly' combined with 'profound slumber,' and both restructure the candle-snuffing action with a parenthetical prisoner comparison. These shared word choices and sentence structures strongly suggest the same paraphraser.

Outputs 1 and 4 share nearly identical phrasing and structure: both use 'primary tenant', 'passed away', 'exact duplicate', and express the philosopher quote in a casual reflective manner with similar wording ('always in plentiful supply' vs 'always an abundance of elderly women'). Their tone, vocabulary choices, and sentence constructions are consistently parallel throughout.

Outputs 1 and 4 share multiple distinctive word choices and structural patterns: both use 'lodgers' (instead of 'boarders'), both describe the young woman as 'bothersome,' both use 'clear the breakfast dishes,' both describe K.'s expression as suggesting he 'anticipated' the invitation and found it 'consistent with'/'aligned with' the disruption caused by the lodgers, and both end with the idea that Fräulein Montag's involvement made the meal repulsive/tainted. These parallel lexical and structural choices suggest they were produced by the same paraphrasing system.

Outputs 0 and 4 share the same sentence structure and nearly identical phrasing: both begin with 'Conseil believed he spotted,' both use 'three prominent ridges running' (with only minor variation), both express disappointment at not seeing the creature, and both end with loggerheads 'distinguished/distinguishable by their lengthy/long shells.' The phrase 'prominent ridges' is unique to these two outputs, and the overall flow and word choices are most similar between them.

Outputs 0 and 3 share nearly identical sentence structure and phrasing throughout: both begin with 'Heidi [adverb] responded to the unfamiliar command,' both continue with 'As she descended the stairs in her elegant dress,' and both end with 'I've never seen you wearing your [X] coat with the [X] buttons before. You look so distinguished!' The differences are only minor lexical substitutions (e.g., 'immediately' vs 'quickly,' 'shiny' vs 'silver,' 'cried' vs 'exclaimed'), which is characteristic of the same paraphrasing system producing slight variations.

human_par3: The hours passed slowly. It must have been half past eleven when he distinctly heard someone moving in the room next to his. The footsteps were light and furtive. Hadn’t Christine gone to bed? Without thinking what he was doing, Raoul dressed quickly, taking care to make no noise. Then, ready for anything, he waited. But ready for what? He had no idea! His heart missed a beat when he heard Christine’s door creak on its hinges. Where could she be going at this time of night when everyone in Perros was asleep? He half opened his door and in a patch of moonlight glimpsed the white figure of Christine gliding cautiously along the corridor. She reached the stairs and went down them. Above her, he leaned over the banister. Suddenly he heard two voices talking urgently. He caught the words: ‘Don’t lose the key.’ It was Mme Tricard’s voice. Then the door overlooking the harbour opened and then closed. Everything went quiet once more. Raoul hurried back to his room, ran to the window and opened it. The white figure of Christine stood on the deserted jetty.
llama_31_8b: Time dragged on until eleven-thirty, when Raoul became aware of someone stealthily moving around the room next to his. He inferred that Christine must be out of her bed because he hadn't seen her there. Without thinking, Raoul got dressed as quietly as possible so as not to make any noise. He then waited with bated breath, although he wasn't quite sure what he was waiting for. His heart skipped a beat as he heard the soft creaking of Christine's door opening very slowly. Raoul couldn't fathom why Christine was slipping downstairs at that hour, when the entire town of Perros seemed to be asleep. He carefully opened his door and caught a glimpse of Christine's white figure carefully making her way down the hallway under the moonlight. She was heading down to the staircase, and Raoul leaned over the banister in an attempt to listen in more closely. He managed to pick up only two hushed conversations and caught one distinct phrase - "Don't lose the key" - which seemed to be spoken by the landlady. He soon realized that the door to the waterfront had swung open and then locked shut again, and everything fell silent once more. In a rush, Raoul hurried back to his room and quickly opened the window. To his surprise, he spotted Christine alone on the deserted dock.
gemini_25_flash: Time seemed to crawl by. Around eleven-thirty, he clearly heard soft, stealthy footsteps in the adjacent room. Christine, he realized, wasn't in her bed! Without a second thought, Raoul quietly got dressed. He then waited, though he wasn't sure what he was waiting for. His heart pounded when he heard Christine's door creak open. Where could she be headed so late, with everyone in Perros asleep? He softly opened his own door and saw her pale shape discreetly moving down the moonlit hallway. She reached the stairs, and as she descended, Raoul peered over the railing. He overheard two hushed voices. He only caught one phrase: "Don't misplace the key." It was the landlady's voice. A door leading to the waterfront opened and then closed, creating silence. Raoul hurried back to his room and quickly opened the window. He saw Christine's white form on the empty dock.
dipper_lowmed: The hours passed very slowly. It was about half-past eleven when Raoul was distinctly aware of quiet, furtive footsteps in the next room. Christine was not in bed! Without thinking, he dressed, taking care not to make any noise, and waited expectantly. What he expected he did not know, but his heart leapt within him when he heard Christine’s door slowly open on its hinges, and her white figure cautiously making its way down the corridor into the moonlight. She reached the staircase and, as she descended, Raoul leaned over the banister and heard two hurried voices, and only one sentence: “Don’t lose the key.” It was his landlady’s voice. Then, as someone opened the door on to the quayside and locked it again, everything went quiet, and Raoul rushed back into his room and quickly opened his window. The white figure of Christine stood in the moonlight on the deserted quayside.

The hours passed very slowly, but at about half-past eleven he distinctly heard the sound of stealthy footsteps in the room next to his. Christine was not in her bed, then. Without thinking, Raoul dressed, and without making a sound, he waited with expectations. He knew not what he expected, but he suddenly heard Christine's door slowly turn on its hinges. Where could she be going at that hour, when all Perros was sleeping? He quietly opened his door, and as he leaned out the door, he caught a glimpse of her white figure cautiously slipping along the corridor in the moonlight, she reached the staircase, and as she ascended, he leaned over the banister and heard two voices, he caught one word: "Don't lose the key," it was the landlady's voice.

human_par3: 'In that case I will go to her,' cried Arkady, with a fresh rush of magnanimous feeling, and he jumped up from his seat. ' I will explain to her that she has no need to be ashamed before me.'

gemini_25_flash: "I'll go see her myself!" Arkady declared, overcome by a renewed sense of generosity, as he leaped from his seat. "I need to go and tell her there's no reason for her to avoid me."
dipper_lowmed: ‘Then let me go and see her,’ Arkady cried with another access of magnanimity, leaping from his chair. ‘ Yes, let me go and explain to her why she shouldn’t avoid me.’

human_par3: K. had just reached the door again when there was a knock. It was the maid, who announced that Fräulein Montag would like a word or two with Herr K. and that she accordingly begged him to come to the dining room, where she was waiting for him. K. listened pensively to the message, then he turned an almost mocking eye on the startled Frau Grubach. His look seemed to say that he had long foreseen this invitation of Fräulein Montag's, and that it accorded very well with all the persecution he had had to endure that Sunday morning from Frau Grubach's boarders. He sent the maid back with the information that he would come at once, then went to his wardrobe to change his coat, and in answer to Frau Grubach, who was softly lamenting over the behavior of the importunate Fräulein Montag, had nothing to say but to request her to remove his breakfast tray. "Why, you've scarcely touched anything," said Frau Grubach. " Oh, do take it away," cried K., it seemed to him as if Fräulein Montag were somehow mixed up with the food and made it nauseating.
llama_31_8b: As K stood in the doorway, a knock at the door interrupted him, announced by the housemaid. She informed him that Fräulein Montag wanted to speak with him and requested that he meet her in the dining room. The maid relayed this message to K, who looked at Frau Grubach with a sense of impatience, seeming to suggest that he had been expecting an invitation from Fräulein Montag and that it only added to the already frustrating interactions he was having with Frau Grubach's other boarders that morning. After nodding to the maid, telling her he'd be right there, K headed to his wardrobe to change. While doing so, he replied to Frau Grubach's complaining by asking her to clear the breakfast dishes, seemingly irritated that Fräulein Montag's involvement in the situation was somehow tied to K's growing annoyance at the other boarders. Frau Grubach pointed out that he hadn't eaten much, and K tersely told her to just remove the dishes, the suggestion that Fräulein Montag was somehow at the heart of the problem apparent in his tone.
gemini_25_flash: K. had just gotten back to the door when someone knocked. It was the maid, who informed him that Miss Montag wanted to speak with him and asked him to meet her in the dining room, where she was waiting. K. listened thoughtfully to the maid, then gave a an almost contemptuous look to a surprised Mrs. Grubach. His expression suggested he had been anticipating Miss Montag's invitation and that it aligned perfectly with the general irritation he was enduring from Mrs. Grubach's lodgers this Sunday morning. He sent the maid back to say he would be right there, then went to his wardrobe to change his jacket. To Mrs. Grubach, who was quietly grumbling about the bothersome young woman, he only asked her to clear the breakfast dishes. "But you've barely eaten anything," Mrs. Grubach remarked. "Oh, just take it away," K. exclaimed; he felt as though Miss Montag was somehow involved in everything, making it all repulsive.

As K. approached the door, a knock announced the maid's arrival. She conveyed Fräulein Montag's wish to speak with him and asked him to meet her in the dining room. human_par3: As to marine mammals, I thought, in passing the entrance of the Adriatic, that I saw two or three cachalots, furnished with one dorsal fin, of the genus physetera, some dolphins of the genus globicephala, peculiar to the Mediterranean, the back part of the head being marked like a zebra with small lines; also, a dozen of seals, with white bellies and black hair, known by the name of monks, and which really have the air of a Dominican; they are about three yards in length. As to zoöphytes, for some instants I was able to admire a beautiful orange galeolaria, which had fastened itself to the port panel; it held on by a long filament, and was divided into an infinity of branches, terminated by the finest lace which could ever have been woven by the rivals of Arachnebk herself.

From my own vantage point, I simply noticed a few loggerhead turtles, distinguished by their long shells.

As for me, the only turtles I observed were a few loggerheads, identifiable by their elongated shells.

The hours passed very slowly. It was about half-past eleven when Raoul was distinctly aware of quiet, furtive footsteps in the next room. Christine was not in bed! Without thinking, he dressed, taking care not to make any noise, and waited expectantly. What he expected he did not know, but his heart leapt within him when he heard Christine’s door slowly open on its hinges, and her white figure cautiously making its way down the corridor into the moonlight. She reached the staircase and, as she descended, Raoul leaned over the banister and heard two hurried voices, and only one sentence: “Don’t lose the key.” It was his landlady’s voice. Then, as someone opened the door on to the quayside and locked it again, everything went quiet, and Raoul rushed back into his room and quickly opened his window. The white figure of Christine stood in the moonlight on the deserted quayside.

"No, she's not here, you old lunatic! " Ivan shouted at him angrily. " Here, he's fainting! Water! A towel! Make haste, Smerdyakov!"

The man was so exhausted that he didn't even appreciate the fresh, clean sheets. He extinguished his candle by pinching it out with his nostril, like prisoners are known to do, and then, still fully clothed, collapsed onto the bed and instantly drifted into a profound slumber.

K. had just gotten back to the door when someone knocked. It was the maid, who informed him that Miss Montag wanted to speak with him and asked him to meet her in the dining room, where she was waiting. K. listened thoughtfully to the maid, then gave a an almost contemptuous look to a surprised Mrs. Grubach. His expression suggested he had been anticipating Miss Montag's invitation and that it aligned perfectly with the general irritation he was enduring from Mrs. Grubach's lodgers this Sunday morning. He sent the maid back to say he would be right there, then went to his wardrobe to change his jacket. To Mrs. Grubach, who was quietly grumbling about the bothersome young woman, he only asked her to clear the breakfast dishes. "But you've barely eaten anything," Mrs. Grubach remarked. "Oh, just take it away," K. exclaimed; he felt as though Miss Montag was somehow involved in everything, making it all repulsive.

As to marine mammals, I thought, in passing the entrance of the Adriatic, that I saw two or three cachalots, furnished with one dorsal fin, of the genus physetera, some dolphins of the genus globicephala, peculiar to the Mediterranean, the back part of the head being marked like a zebra with small lines; also, a dozen of seals, with white bellies and black hair, known by the name of monks, and which really have the air of a Dominican; they are about three yards in length. As to zoöphytes, for some instants I was able to admire a beautiful orange galeolaria, which had fastened itself to the port panel; it held on by a long filament, and was divided into an infinity of branches, terminated by the finest lace which could ever have been woven by the rivals of Arachnebk herself.

Time dragged on until eleven-thirty, when Raoul became aware of someone stealthily moving around the room next to his. He inferred that Christine must be out of her bed because he hadn't seen her there. Without thinking, Raoul got dressed as quietly as possible so as not to make any noise. He then waited with bated breath, although he wasn't quite sure what he was waiting for. His heart skipped a beat as he heard the soft creaking of Christine's door opening very slowly. Raoul couldn't fathom why Christine was slipping downstairs at that hour, when the entire town of Perros seemed to be asleep. He carefully opened his door and caught a glimpse of Christine's white figure carefully making her way down the hallway under the moonlight. She was heading down to the staircase, and Raoul leaned over the banister in an attempt to listen in more closely. He managed to pick up only two hushed conversations and caught one distinct phrase - "Don't lose the key" - which seemed to be spoken by the landlady. He soon realized that the door to the waterfront had swung open and then locked shut again, and everything fell silent once more. In a rush, Raoul hurried back to his room and quickly opened the window. To his surprise, he spotted Christine alone on the deserted dock.
